verses.text: And Solomon made affinity with Pharaoh king of Egypt, and took Pharaoh's daughter, and brought her into the city of David, until he had made an end of building his own house, and the house of the LORD, and the wall of Jerusalem round about. Only the people sacrificed in high places, because there was no house built unto the name of the LORD, until those days. And Solomon loved the LORD, walking in the statutes of David his father: only he sacrificed and burnt incense in high places. Guidance: A three-part introduction to Solomon's reign as it begins. (1) The political marriage. 'Solomon made affinity (hitchatten — became son-in-law) with Pharaoh king of Egypt.' Marrying the daughter of the Twenty-first Dynasty Pharaoh (probably Siamun) was a major diplomatic achievement; Egypt rarely gave Pharaohs' daughters to foreign kings. The marriage signals Israel's standing as a regional power. But the narrator's tone is ambivalent. Deuteronomy 7:3-4 had warned against marrying foreign women lest they turn the king's heart from the LORD. Deuteronomy 17:17 specifically forbids the king to multiply wives. Solomon's first political act is technically a violation of both. The chapter is setting up a tension: a king who 'loved the LORD' (v3) is simultaneously building a foreign-marriage portfolio that will eventually destroy him. (2) The high places. Until Solomon built the temple, there was no central sanctuary; the tabernacle was at Gibeon, the ark was on the southeast hill of Jerusalem. The Israelites worshiped at various open-air sanctuary sites — bamot — high places. The narrator notes 'there was no house built unto the name of the LORD until those days' as an explanation: high-place worship is an interim arrangement. The chapter is preparing us for the temple-building of chapter 6 as the resolution to the high-places problem. (3) Solomon's love. 'Solomon loved the LORD, walking in the statutes of David his father.' This is the chapter's headline characterization of the young king. The verb ahav (love) here connects to the deuteronomic command to love the LORD with all the heart (Deut 6:5). For the moment, that love is real. The closing 'only he sacrificed and burnt incense in high places' reminds us that the love is imperfect in its expression. The chapter is showing us the young Solomon at his theological best: a king who loves the LORD; whose institutional setup is still incomplete; whose foreign-marriage strategy will eventually undo him; whose early devotional life is the high point from which his later compromises will descend.

And the king went to Gibeon to sacrifice there; for that was the great high place: a thousand burnt offerings did Solomon offer upon that altar. In Gibeon the LORD appeared to Solomon in a dream by night: and God said, Ask what I shall give thee. Guidance: The Gibeon offering and the night-dream. Gibeon — a Levitical town about six miles northwest of Jerusalem — was 'the great high place' (ha-bamah ha-gedolah). According to 1 Chronicles 16:39 and 21:29, the tabernacle of Moses and the bronze altar of Bezalel were located there during this period (the ark having been brought to Jerusalem by David, but the rest of the tabernacle's furniture remaining at Gibeon). Solomon's offering at Gibeon is therefore not a syncretistic act at a generic high place; it is a major liturgical event at the principal national sanctuary outside Jerusalem. A thousand burnt offerings — eleph olot — is an enormous quantity, signaling either an extended liturgical gathering or a literal thousand head of cattle and sheep slaughtered in tribute. The chapter is showing us the young king investing massive resources in worship. The LORD's response is the dream-theophany. 'God said, Ask what I shall give thee.' The Hebrew she'al mah etten lakh — ask, what shall I give thee. This is one of the great open-ended offers in Scripture. Few characters in the Old Testament are given such a blank check. The chapter is testing what Solomon's heart will reach for when the LORD himself invites the request. The reach will reveal the man.

The chapter is showing us the young king investing massive resources in worship. The LORD's response is the dream-theophany. 'God said, Ask what I shall give thee.' The Hebrew she'al mah etten lakh — ask, what shall I give thee. This is one of the great open-ended offers in Scripture. Few characters in the Old Testament are given such a blank check. And Solomon said, Thou hast shewed unto thy servant David my father great mercy, according as he walked before thee in truth, and in righteousness, and in uprightness of heart with thee; and thou hast kept for him this great kindness, that thou hast given him a son to sit on his throne, as it is this day. And now, O LORD my God, thou hast made thy servant king instead of David my father: and I am but a little child: I know not how to go out or come in. And thy servant is in the midst of thy people which thou hast chosen, a great people, that cannot be numbered nor counted for multitude. Give therefore thy servant an understanding heart to judge thy people, that I may discern between good and bad: for who is able to judge this thy so great a people? Guidance: Solomon's answer — one of the most remarkable petitions ever offered to God by a king. The structure is exemplary. (1) He begins not with himself but with God's faithfulness to his father. 'Thou hast shewed unto thy servant David my father great mercy.' He recalls David's life of truth, righteousness, and uprightness — and the LORD's covenant kindness in giving David a son to succeed him. The petition begins with theological gratitude before personal request. (2) He acknowledges his own inadequacy. 'I am but a little child: I know not how to go out or come in.' The Hebrew na'ar qaton — small youth. Solomon is probably about twenty years old (he was born around 990 BC, ascended around 970 BC). The phrase 'go out or come in' is military-administrative language for governing-action. He confesses: I do not know how to do this job. The chapter is showing us a young king of great wisdom precisely because he begins by confessing he has none. The first act of wisdom is the confession of foolishness. (3) He frames the scope of the work. 'Thy servant is in the midst of thy people which thou hast chosen, a great people.' Not 'my people' — 'thy people which thou hast chosen.' The throne is held in trust for the LORD. The kingdom does not belong to Solomon; it belongs to the LORD. (4) The petition: 'Give therefore thy servant an understanding heart to judge thy people, that I may discern between good and bad.' Hebrew lev shomea — a hearing/listening heart. He asks not for cleverness (chochmah does not appear here directly) but for receptive moral discernment. The capacity to distinguish good from evil in concrete cases. (5) The closing rhetorical question: 'Who is able to judge this thy so great a people?' Solomon ends as he began — in humility. The chapter is offering us a model of how to ask God for what we need: gratitude for past mercies, confession of present inadequacy, recognition that the work belongs to God, request for the specific capacity needed for the assignment, humble acknowledgement that we do not naturally possess that capacity. Christians have read this prayer for centuries as a template for every leader's first prayer.

And the speech pleased the Lord, that Solomon had asked this thing. And God said unto him, Because thou hast asked this thing, and hast not asked for thyself long life; neither hast asked riches for thyself, nor hast asked the life of thine enemies; but hast asked for thyself understanding to discern judgment; Behold, I have done according to thy words: lo, I have given thee a wise and an understanding heart; so that there was none like thee before thee, neither after thee shall any arise like unto thee. And I have also given thee that which thou hast not asked, both riches, and honour: so that there shall not be any among the kings like unto thee all thy days. And if thou wilt walk in my ways, to keep my statutes and my commandments, as thy father David did walk, then I will lengthen thy days. Guidance: The LORD's reply. 'The speech pleased the Lord' — the verb yitav — God smiled on the request. The LORD then enumerates what Solomon could have asked for but did not. (1) Long life — the standard royal anxiety. (2) Riches — the standard royal greed. (3) The life of his enemies — the standard royal vengeance. The chapter is showing us what most people would have asked for in Solomon's place. Solomon asked for none of these. He asked for moral discernment to serve his calling. The LORD's reply is generous beyond what was asked. (a) The petition is granted: 'I have given thee a wise and an understanding heart; so that there was none like thee before thee, neither after thee shall any arise like unto thee.' Solomon's wisdom will be unique in Israel's history. (b) The unrequested blessings are also granted: 'riches, and honour: so that there shall not be any among the kings like unto thee all thy days.' Solomon will be the wealthiest king Israel ever sees. The principle here is foundational: when we ask for what God most wants to give, He gives us what we did not ask for as well. Jesus echoes the principle in Matt 6:33 — 'Seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you.' (c) The conditional bonus: 'If thou wilt walk in my ways… then I will lengthen thy days.' Long life is offered, but conditioned on obedience. Solomon will, eventually, fail this condition; he dies in his early sixties — not the long life he might have had. The chapter is teaching us that some of God's gifts come unconditionally with what we asked for; others remain conditional on faithfulness. The wisdom and riches were given; the longevity was offered if Solomon kept walking. The chapter is honest about both kinds of divine generosity.

(a) The petition is granted: 'I have given thee a wise and an understanding heart; so that there was none like thee before thee, neither after thee shall any arise like unto thee.' Solomon's wisdom will be unique in Israel's history. (b) The unrequested blessings are also granted: 'riches, and honour: so that there shall not be any among the kings like unto thee all thy days.' Solomon will be the wealthiest king Israel ever sees. The principle here is foundational: when we ask for what God most wants to give, He gives us what we did not ask for as well. Jesus echoes the principle in Matt 6:33 — 'Seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you.' (c) The conditional bonus: 'If thou wilt walk in my ways… then I will lengthen thy days.' Long life is offered, but conditioned on obedience. Solomon will, eventually, fail this condition; he dies in his early sixties — not the long life he might have had. The chapter is teaching us that some of God's gifts come unconditionally with what we asked for; others remain conditional on faithfulness. The wisdom and riches were given; the longevity was offered if Solomon kept walking. The chapter is honest about both kinds of divine generosity.

And Solomon awoke; and, behold, it was a dream. And he came to Jerusalem, and stood before the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and offered up burnt offerings, and offered peace offerings, and made a feast to all his servants. Guidance: Awakening, return, response. 'Solomon awoke; and, behold, it was a dream.' The verb va-yiqatz — and he was awake. The Hebrew vehinnei chalom — and behold, a dream. The narrator is careful: it was a dream, not a hallucination; the divine encounter happened in the standard biblical medium of revelatory sleep (Gen 28, Gen 37, Matt 1-2). Solomon returns to Jerusalem and goes immediately to the ark — the visible symbol of the LORD's covenant presence. He offers burnt offerings and peace offerings (shelamim — fellowship offerings, eaten in joint celebration). Then he 'made a feast to all his servants.' The chapter is showing us the right response to a divine encounter: worship at the place of the LORD's presence, then a celebration shared with the household. Solomon translates the private dream into public liturgy and communal joy. The peace offerings would have been roasted and eaten with the staff; the king's blessing extends to the king's people. The chapter is teaching us that grace received personally is meant to be celebrated communally. The very wisdom Solomon will exercise in the next paragraph is grounded here — at the ark, in worship, in feast.

Then came there two women, that were harlots, unto the king, and stood before him. And the one woman said, O my lord, I and this woman dwell in one house; and I was delivered of a child with her in the house. And it came to pass the third day after that I was delivered, that this woman was delivered also: and we were together; there was no stranger with us in the house, save we two in the house. And this woman's child died in the night; because she overlaid it. And she arose at midnight, and took my son from beside me, while thine handmaid slept, and laid it in her bosom, and laid her dead child in my bosom. And when I rose in the morning to give my child suck, behold, it was dead: but when I had considered it in the morning, behold, it was not my son, which I did bear. And the other woman said, Nay; but the living is my son, and the dead is thy son. And this said, No; but the dead is thy son, and the living is my son. Thus they spake before the king. Guidance: The famous trial scene. Two women — harlots (zonot — prostitutes; their social standing is at the very bottom of ancient Israelite society). They live together in one house with no witnesses; both give birth within three days of each other. One mother accidentally smothers her child in the night ('she overlaid it') and switches the dead infant for the other's living one. The grieving mother realizes the switch at dawn when she discovers in better light that the dead infant in her arms is not her child. The narrative detail 'when I had considered it in the morning' (ke'asher etbonen elaiv ba-boqer) is touching — she examined the small body carefully and saw it was not the one she had borne. The case comes to Solomon. There are no witnesses; there is no biological test; there is only the word of one woman against the other. The chapter is presenting Solomon with the hardest kind of judicial case: he-said-she-said with a living child as the stakes. Two things are crucial to notice. (1) The two women are harlots — society's lowest class. Yet they have access to the king. Solomon is hearing their case personally. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's anointed king is available to the poorest and most disreputable. (2) The case is not over money or property; it is over a baby. The infant cannot speak; he is the most vulnerable kind of plaintiff. The king who has just been given a 'hearing heart' is asked, on his first major case, to hear what cannot speak.

Then said the king, The one saith, This is my son that liveth, and thy son is the dead: and the other saith, Nay; but thy son is the dead, and my son is the living. And the king said, Bring me a sword. And they brought a sword before the king. And the king said, Divide the living child in two, and give half to the one, and half to the other. Then spake the woman whose the living child was unto the king, for her bowels yearned upon her son, and she said, O my lord, give her the living child, and in no wise slay it. But the other said, Let it be neither mine nor thine, but divide it. Then the king answered and said, Give her the living child, and in no wise slay it: she is the mother thereof. Guidance: The verdict — one of the most famous judicial moments in human history. Solomon's method is psychological and brilliant. (1) He restates the case neutrally — naming the dispute without taking sides. (2) He calls for a sword. The sword is a shock; no one in court expected it. (3) He proposes the absurd remedy: cut the baby in half, give each woman a piece. (4) He watches the reactions. The real mother's response is recorded in unforgettable language: 'her bowels yearned upon her son.' The Hebrew nikhmeru rachameha — 'her compassions burned hot.' The womb-word rechem, root of mercy-language, here erupts. She would rather lose the child to her rival than see him killed. 'O my lord, give her the living child, and in no wise slay it.' The other woman, the lying mother, responds with cold spite: 'Let it be neither mine nor thine, but divide it.' If she cannot have the child, neither will the rival. The chapter is showing us how the truth of a mother's heart is unmaskable. Solomon does not need witnesses or biological tests; he needs only to set up a situation where the real love and the false love must reveal themselves. The fake claimant can keep up the pretense of motherhood until the price of motherhood becomes the child's death; the real mother breaks the moment her child is threatened. (5) The verdict: 'Give her the living child… she is the mother thereof.' The Hebrew hi immo — she is his mother. Solomon does not require a confession from the liar; the mother is identified by her love. The chapter is teaching us a profound truth about identification: real love proves itself by what it is willing to lose. The fake mother would rather kill than yield; the real mother yields rather than kill. There is a Christian application — the cross — but it is also an everyday principle for parents, teachers, ministers, anyone who claims to love what they shepherd: the test is what you are willing to lose to keep them alive.

(5) The verdict: 'Give her the living child… she is the mother thereof.' The Hebrew hi immo — she is his mother. Solomon does not require a confession from the liar; the mother is identified by her love. The chapter is teaching us a profound truth about identification: real love proves itself by what it is willing to lose. The fake mother would rather kill than yield; the real mother yields rather than kill. There is a Christian application — the cross — but it is also an everyday principle for parents, teachers, ministers, anyone who claims to love what they shepherd: the test is what you are willing to lose to keep them alive.

And all Israel heard of the judgment which the king had judged; and they feared the king: for they saw that the wisdom of God was in him, to do judgment. Guidance: The verdict's public effect. 'All Israel heard of the judgment.' Word spreads. The court ruling becomes a national story; the king's wisdom becomes proverbial within his own generation. 'They feared the king.' Fear (yir'ah) here is not terror but reverent recognition — the same word used for the fear of the LORD. The people honor Solomon precisely because they see something beyond him in him. 'They saw that the wisdom of God was in him, to do judgment.' Hebrew chochmat Elohim be-qirbo — the wisdom of God in his midst. The chapter's closing line gives us the theological interpretation. Solomon's wisdom is not his own intellectual brightness; it is divine wisdom temporarily resident in a human king. The Gibeon prayer is being fulfilled in the courtroom. The chapter is ending where it began — with the LORD's gift in Solomon. The young king who asked for a hearing heart has used it; the people see God-given wisdom at work and they revere the office. The chapter is establishing the pattern that will define the first half of Solomon's reign: a king through whom divine wisdom flows into national life. The reputation will spread beyond Israel — by chapter 10 the Queen of Sheba will come to verify it. But it begins here, in the courtroom, with two harlots and a baby. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD often establishes great reputations through small cases handled with extraordinary care. The most disreputable plaintiffs received the king's full hearing; the most vulnerable victim received protection; the bereaved received vindication. From this seed of small justice, a kingdom's reputation grows.
